elements.sent-2411-001: Suppose that $h$ is an instance of the Gaussian free field (GFF) on a simply connected domain $D \subseteq \C$ and $x,y \in \partial D$ are distinct.
elements.sent-2411-002: Fix $\kappa \in (0,4)$ and for each $\theta \in \R$ let $\eta_\theta$ be the flow line of $h$ from $x$ to $y$.
elements.sent-2411-003: Recall that for $\theta_1 < \theta_2$ the fan $\fan(\theta_1,\theta_2)$ of flow lines of $h$ from $x$ to $y$ is the closure of the union of $\eta_\theta$ as $\theta$ varies in any fixed countable dense subset of $[\theta_1,\theta_2]$.
elements.sent-2411-004: We show that the adjacency graph of components of $D \setminus \fan(\theta_1,\theta_2)$ is a.s.\ connected, meaning it a.s.\ holds that for every pair $U,V$ of components there exist components $U_1,\ldots,U_n$ so that $U_1 = U$, $U_n = V$, and $\partial U_i \cap \partial U_{i+1 \neq \emptyset$ for each $1 \leq i \leq n-1$.}
elements.sent-2411-005: We further show that $\fan(\theta_1,\theta_2)$ a.s.\ determines the flow lines used in its construction.
elements.sent-2411-006: That is, for each $\theta \in [\theta_1,\theta_2]$ we prove that $\eta_\theta$ is a.s.\ determined by $\fan(\theta_1,\theta_2)$ as a set.
elements.sent-2411-007: The Schramm-Loewner evolution ($\SLE_{\kappa}$, $\kappa > 0$) is a one parameter family of curves which connect two boundary points of a simply connected domain.
elements.sent-2411-008: It was introduced by Schramm in \cite{s2000sle as a candidate to describe the scaling limit of the interfaces of various two-dimensional discrete models from statistical mechanics at criticality.}
elements.sent-2411-009: A number of such convergence results have been proved in the case of planar lattices \cite{lsw2004lerw,s2001cardy,s2010ising,ss2009contours and random planar maps \cite{s2016inventory,kmsw2019bipolar,lsw2017wood,gkmw2018active, gwynne2021convergence,gm2021saw .}}
elements.sent-2411-010: The parameter $\kappa \geq 0$ determines the roughness of an $\SLE_{\kappa}$ curve.
elements.sent-2411-011: An $\SLE_0$ is a smooth curve and $\SLE_{\kappa}$ curves become more fractal as $\kappa$ increases.
elements.sent-2411-012: There are three regimes of $\kappa$ values which exhibit rather different behavior: for $\kappa \in [0,4]$ an $\SLE_{\kappa}$ is a.s.\ simple, for $\kappa \geq 8$ it is a.s.\ space-filling, and for $\kappa \in (4,8)$ it is a.s.\ self-intersecting but not space-filling \cite{rs2005basic .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Furthermore, the a.s.\ dimension of the range of an $\SLE_{\kappa}$ curve is given by $\min(1+\kappa/8 , 2)$ \cite{rs2005basic,beffara2008dimension .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Schramm's original definition of $\SLE_\kappa$ is in terms of the chordal Loewner equation driven by $\sqrt{\kappa B$ where $B$ is a standard Brownian motion and in the intervening years since its introduction several other representations of $\SLE_\kappa$ have been discovered.}
elements.sent-2411-015: In this work, its coupling with the Gaussian free field (GFF) will be particularly important.
elements.sent-2411-016: Recall that the GFF $h$ on a simply connected domain $D \subseteq \C$ is the Gaussian field with covariance given by the Green's function $G$ for $\Delta$ on $D$.
elements.sent-2411-017: Since $G(x,y) \sim -\log|x-y|$ as $x \to y$, the GFF has infinite variance at points and so takes values in the space of distributions on $D$ rather than a space of functions.
elements.sent-2411-018: Nevertheless, it is very close to being a function in the sense that $h$ takes values in the Sobolev space $H^{-\epsilon (D)$ for every $\epsilon > 0$ but not~$L^2$.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Consequently, it exhibits many of the features of a function (but with an extra complication to reflect that it is only distribution valued).
elements.sent-2411-020: For example, it was shown in \cite{she2016zipper (building on \cite{schramm2013contour ) that it is possible to make sense of the flow lines of the formal vector field $e^{i (h(\eta(t))/\chi + \theta )}$, i.e., solutions to the $\text{ODE}$ \begin{equation}\eta'(t) = e^{i ( h(\eta(t))/\chi + \theta)} \quad for \quad t>0 \quadwhere\quad \chi = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\kappa}}-\frac{\sqrt{\kappa}}{2} \quadfor\quad \kappa \in (0,4)\end{equation} and they are $\SLE_\kappa$ type curves.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: The theory of how the GFF flow lines behave and interact with each other was developed in \cite{dub2009gff, ms2016imag1,ms2017ig4 .}

The boundary conditions for $h$ are chosen so that the $0$-angle flow line is an $\SLE_1$ and the values of $h$ are shown in grayscale. {\bf Left:} Flow lines with different angles are shown with different colors. {\bf Right:} All of the flow lines are shown in white. In Theorem~\ref{thm:fan_determines_flow_lines}, we prove that one can recover the flow lines which make up $\fan$ from $\fan$ (as a closed set). elements.sent-2411-026: For $\theta \in \R$, we let $\eta_{\theta}$ be the flow line of $h$ of angle $\theta$ from $0$ to $\infty$.

elements.sent-2411-045: The connectivity of the adjacency graph of complementary components is a useful topological property which has many other applications in the study of such random fractals.
elements.sent-2411-046: Let us describe one such motivation, which comes from the relationship between $\SLE_\kappa$-type curves and Liouville quantum gravity (LQG) surfaces.
elements.sent-2411-047: Recall that an $\text{LQG}$ surface is a random two-dimensional Riemannian manifold which is formally described by the metric tensor \begin{equation}\label{eqn:lqg_metric_tensor} e^{\gamma h(z)}\left( dx^2 + dy^2\right) \quad\text{for}\quad z = x + iy\end{equation} where $dx^2 + dy^2$ is the Euclidean metric on a domain $D \subseteq \C$, $h$ is (some form of) the $\text{GFF}$ on $D$, and $\gamma \in (0,2]$ is a parameter.

elements.sent-2411-072: We are now going to review some basic facts about Bessel processes.
elements.sent-2411-073: We refer the reader to \cite{revuz2013continuous for more details.}
elements.sent-2411-075: The starting point for the construction of the law of a Bessel process of dimension $\delta$ ($\BES^\delta$) is the construction of the law of the so-called square Bessel process of dimension $\delta$ $(\BESQ^{\delta )$.}
elements.sent-2411-076: The law of a $\BESQ^{\delta}$ is described by the SDE \begin{equation}\label{eqn:bessel_square_sde} dY_t = \delta dt + 2\sqrt{Y_t} dB_t,\quad Y_0 = y_0 >0,\end{equation} where $B$ is a standard Brownian motion.
elements.sent-2411-077: Standard results for SDEs imply that there is a unique strong solution to~\eqref{eqn:bessel_square_sde , at least up until the first time that the process hits $0$.}
elements.sent-2411-078: In the case that $\delta>0$, there is a unique strong solution to~\eqref{eqn:bessel_square_sde for all $t \geq 0$ and the solution remains non-negative for all times a.s.}
elements.sent-2411-079: A process $X$ has the law of a $\BES^{\delta}$ process if it can be expressed as $X = \sqrt{Y}$ where $Y$ is a $\BESQ^{\delta}$.
elements.sent-2411-080: By applying It\^o's formula, we obtain that $X$ solves the SDE \begin{equation}\label{eqn:bessel_sde} dX_t = \frac{a}{X_t}dt + dB_t,\quad X_0 = x\geq 0 \quad\text{where}\quad a = \frac{\delta-1}{2},\end{equation} at least up until it hits $0$.
elements.sent-2411-081: If $\delta \in (0,2)$, then $X_t$ will a.s.\ hit and be instantaneously reflected at~$0$; if $\delta = 2$, then $X_t$ will a.s.\ not hit $0$ but will get arbitrarily close to $0$; and if $\delta > 2$, then a.s.\ $\lim_{t \to \infty X_t = \infty$.}
elements.sent-2411-082: An important property of $\BES^{\delta}$ processes is that they satisfy Brownian scaling, i.e., if $X$ is a $\BES^{\delta}$ then so is the process $t \mapsto r^{-1 X_{r^2 t}$ for each $r>0$ fixed.}
elements.sent-2411-083: When $\delta \in (1,2)$, the process solves~\eqref{eqn:bessel_sde in integrated form for all $t \geq 0$ hence is a semimartingale.}
elements.sent-2411-084: When $\delta = 1$, the process is equal in distribution to $|B|$ where $B$ is a standard Brownian motion and solves~\eqref{eqn:bessel_sde with an extra correction coming from the local time of $X$ at $0$ so is also a semimartingale.}
elements.sent-2411-085: When $\delta \in (0,1)$, the process does not solve~\eqref{eqn:bessel_sde when hitting $0$ in integrated form and is not a semimartingale.}
elements.sent-2411-086: In order to make sense of it as a solution to~\eqref{eqn:bessel_sde , we need to make a so-called principal value correction (see \cite{s2009cle for more details).}}

elements.sent-2411-856: Next, we prove that the fan $\F(\tht_1, \tht_2)$ is locally connected (with respect to the subspace topology).
elements.sent-2411-857: A topological space $X$ is locally connected at $x \in X$ if every neighborhood (in $X$) of $x$ contains a connected open neighborhood of $x$ (see e.g.\ \cite[\S25]{munkres or \cite[\S27]{willard ).}}
elements.sent-2411-858: The space $X$ itself is called locally connected or connected im kleinen if it is locally connected at every point $x \in X$ or connected im kleinen at every point $x \in X$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-859: While local connectedness and connectedness im kleinen at a single point $x$ are not equivalent, a space $X$ is locally connected if and only if it is connected im kleinen \cite[Theorem~27.16]{willard .}
elements.sent-2411-860: Before showing the fan is locally connected, we prove two auxiliary lemmas.
elements.sent-2411-861: The proof of the first is adapted from \cite{nd-stackexchange .}
elements.sent-2411-862: Let $F \subseteq \C$ be closed and let $K \subseteq F$ be compact.
elements.sent-2411-863: If $C$ is a connected component of $K$ which does not intersect $\del_F K$, the boundary of $K$ in $F$, then $C$ is a connected component of $F$.
elements.sent-2411-864: The quasicomponent of a point $x$ in $K$ is the intersection of all sets $D$ which are clopen in $K$ and contain $x$ \cite[\S6.1]{engelking .}
elements.sent-2411-865: Since $K$ is a compact Hausdorff space, its connected components and quasicomponents agree \cite[Theorem~6.1.23]{engelking .}
elements.sent-2411-866: This means that $C$ is the intersection of all sets $D$ which are clopen in $K$ and which contain $C$.
elements.sent-2411-867: Any point $b \in \del_F K$ is not in $C$, so there must exist a set $U_b$, which is clopen in $K$, contains $b$, and is disjoint from $C$.
elements.sent-2411-868: The sets $U_b$ for $b \in \del_F K$ form an open cover of $\del_F K$, and since $K$ is compact, we can find a finite subcover whose union $U$ is clopen (in $K$), contains $\del_F K$ and is disjoint from $C$.
elements.sent-2411-869: Then $C \subseteq K\sm U \subseteq K \sm \del_F K \subseteq K \subseteq F$.
elements.sent-2411-870: Also, $K \sm U$ is open in $K$ and contained in $K \sm \del_F K$, hence is open in $F$.
elements.sent-2411-871: Similarly, $K \sm U$ is closed in $K$ and hence closed in $F$.
elements.sent-2411-872: Therefore any connected superset $D$ of $C$ in $F$ must be contained in $K \sm U$ (since this set is clopen) and hence must be equal to $C$ since $C$ is a connected component of $K$.
